refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3, DP6) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular hot-swappable design for rapid component replacement, (3) predictive condition monitoring to detect degradation early, and (4) comprehensive fault detection and isolation with automated diagnostics—all coordinated to achieve target availability ≥0.97 by minimizing both failure frequency and mean-time-to-repair.

potential_sources: Redundancy strategy (DP1 component) must be decoupled from failover logic to avoid tight coupling between detection and switching mechanisms.
Predictive maintenance (DP3 component) depends on sensor data quality; coupling risk exists if monitoring architecture is not cleanly separated from control logic.
Synchronization between distributed nodes (DP5 component, if adopted) introduces temporal coupling; requires explicit protocol definition to avoid cascading failures.
FDIA system (DP6 component) must not block normal operation; risk of tight coupling between diagnostics and control path if not architecturally isolated.
Integration of four sub-strategies (DP7) risks over-coupling if interfaces between redundancy, modularity, monitoring, and diagnostics are not clearly defined.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 is composite and requires explicit interface contracts between sub-strategies. Recommend decomposing into independent DP layers: (Layer 1) Redundancy & Failover, (Layer 2) Modularity & Replacement, (Layer 3) Monitoring & Prediction, (Layer 4) Diagnostics & Isolation. Each layer should have defined inputs/outputs and minimal cross-layer dependencies.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be procured, integrated, and maintained within cost and schedule constraints; RISK: Redundancy adds weight, power, complexity, and cost—customer acceptance required.

ASSUMPTION: Hot-swappable components are feasible for all critical subsystems; RISK: Some subsystems (e.g., structural, high-voltage power) may not support safe hot-swap; design may require controlled shutdown.

ASSUMPTION: Predictive maintenance models are accurate and calibrated for NGHE operating environment; RISK: Model drift or environmental variation may reduce prediction accuracy, leading to false positives/negatives.
ASSUMPTION: Fault detection latency is acceptable (seconds to minutes); RISK: If detection delay exceeds RTO, availability target may not be met.
ASSUMPTION: Operator response time and spare parts availability are consistent with MTTR targets; RISK: Supply chain delays or operator unavailability may violate availability SLA.
ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is representative of operational conditions; RISK: Short evaluation window may not capture rare failure modes or seasonal variations.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits automated failover and component replacement without manual intervention; RISK: Safety constraints may mandate human approval, increasing MTTR.
ASSUMPTION: Monitoring and diagnostics do not introduce new failure modes (e.g., sensor failures, false alarms); RISK: Monitoring infrastructure itself must be highly reliable.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: (1) Target availability 0.97 applies to [core function X] under [defined operating conditions]; (2) Acceptable downtime per incident and per evaluation period; (3) Definition of 'operational' (e.g., full capacity vs. degraded mode); (4) Constraints on redundancy (cost, weight, power budget); (5) Maintenance model (preventive, predictive, or reactive). Decompose DP7 into a layered architecture with explicit DP definitions for each layer and clear interface contracts. Establish acceptance criteria for each sub-strategy (e.g., failover latency <5s, MTTR <2h, prediction accuracy >90%). Conduct FMEA to identify failure modes not addressed by DP7 and iterate.

im_boundary.name: Safety Services
im_boundary.scope: Centralized availability and fault management subsystem responsible for redundancy coordination, failover orchestration, condition monitoring, fault detection/isolation, and diagnostics across all vehicle and teleoperation subsystems.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound health and performance data from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems; feeds predictive condition monitoring and fault detection logic.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound alerts and automated diagnostics results; triggers failover commands and component replacement notifications.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional coordination: receives operational context from NGHE Solution; sends failover/degraded-mode directives to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Supports remote diagnostics, predictive analytics updates, and coordination of distributed redundancy across networked subsystems.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit: Safety Services is the natural owner of fault detection, diagnostics, and failover orchestration. Redundancy coordination, condition monitoring, and automated diagnostics are core safety functions. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement redundant hardware/software and expose health telemetry, but control logic remains centralized in Safety Services.

Poor fit: Vehicle is a physical platform, not a cross-cutting availability coordinator. Redundancy logic, failover orchestration, and predictive monitoring would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution. Violates separation of concerns; mixes platform-specific mechanics with system-level availability policy.

Poor fit: Teleoperation is a control interface, not a fault management authority. Availability coordination must span both Vehicle and Teleoperation; placing it here creates asymmetric ownership and forces Vehicle to depend on Teleoperation for failover decisions, violating autonomy.

Poor fit: NGHE Solution is the top-level mission orchestrator, not a real-time fault manager. Placing low-latency failover, condition monitoring, and diagnostics here introduces unacceptable latency and couples mission logic to availability mechanics. Diffusion is severe across all subsystems.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: Safety Services is the designated owner of system-level safety, fault tolerance, and availability guarantees. DP7 (integrated availability architecture) is fundamentally a safety and resilience concern, not a mission or platform concern. Centralizing redundancy coordination, failover logic, condition monitoring, and diagnostics in Safety Services ensures coherent policy, rapid response, and clear accountability for the ≥0.97 availability target. Vehicle and Teleoperation remain responsible for implementing redundant hardware/software and exposing health telemetry; Safety Services orchestrates their use.
containment_risks: Risk: Safety Services may become a bottleneck if failover decisions require synchronous consensus across distributed subsystems. Mitigation: Design failover logic with local autonomy (e.g., Vehicle can trigger local redundancy without waiting for Safety Services acknowledgment) and asynchronous coordination.

Risk: Predictive condition monitoring requires deep domain knowledge of Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems. Mitigation: Safety Services defines monitoring interfaces and thresholds; Vehicle and Teleoperation provide domain-specific health metrics and degradation models.

Risk: Network latency may delay fault detection and failover if Safety Services is remote. Mitigation: Implement local fault detection and failover in Vehicle and Teleoperation; Safety Services provides global coordination and predictive analytics.

Risk: Coupling between Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation may increase if redundancy design is not modular. Mitigation: Use well-defined Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces; avoid direct access to internal subsystem state.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Availability ≥0.97 is measurable and verifiable over a defined evaluation period. Safety Services as the centralized owner provides a single point of accountability for monitoring, reporting, and validating the target. Telemetry inbound interface enables continuous collection of health data; SafetyAlert outbound interface enables automated diagnostics and failover event logging. Verification requires: (1) instrumentation of all critical subsystems to feed Telemetry, (2) definition of availability metrics (uptime vs. degraded-mode operation), (3) agreed evaluation period and measurement methodology, (4) baseline and target thresholds documented in acceptance criteria.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: Safety Services owns the integrated availability architecture (redundancy coordination, failover orchestration, condition monitoring, fault detection/isolation, diagnostics). Vehicle and Teleoperation own implementation of redundant hardware/software and exposure of health telemetry. NGHE Solution provides operational context and receives failover/degraded-mode directives. Ownership boundaries are well-defined; however, clarity depends on explicit definition of: (a) what constitutes 'availability' (e.g., full operational readiness vs. degraded-mode capability), (b) which subsystem failures are tolerable vs. mission-critical, (c) failover decision latency requirements, (d) network link reliability assumptions.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 directly addresses FR requirement. The four pillars (redundancy + failover, modular hot-swap, predictive monitoring, fault detection/isolation + diagnostics) are logically sufficient to achieve and maintain ≥0.97 availability by reducing both failure frequency (via redundancy and predictive maintenance) and mean-time-to-repair (via modular design and automated diagnostics). DP is solution-neutral (does not prescribe specific technologies) and measurable against the target. No logical gaps identified between FR and DP.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Predictive condition monitoring logic may diffuse across Safety Services, Vehicle, and Teleoperation if domain-specific health models are not cleanly abstracted. Mitigation: Define a standardized health metric schema in Telemetry interface; Vehicle and Teleoperation provide raw sensor data and degradation models; Safety Services applies predictive algorithms. Ownership: Safety Services owns prediction logic; Vehicle/Teleoperation own domain models.

Risk: Failover orchestration may diffuse if local autonomy is not clearly bounded. If Vehicle or Teleoperation can trigger redundancy independently without Safety Services coordination, availability policy becomes fragmented. Mitigation: Define explicit failover decision rules (e.g., Vehicle triggers local redundancy on sensor failure; Safety Services coordinates global failover if Vehicle becomes unavailable). Document decision authority per failure mode.

Risk: Fault detection and isolation logic may duplicate across Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation if subsystem-level diagnostics are not integrated into the centralized architecture. Mitigation: Safety Services owns system-level fault isolation; Vehicle and Teleoperation own subsystem-level diagnostics. SafetyAlert interface must consolidate all diagnostics into a unified view.

Risk: Network link reliability is a critical dependency for Safety Services to coordinate distributed redundancy. If network latency or packet loss is high, failover decisions may be delayed or inconsistent. Mitigation: Implement local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation; Safety Services provides asynchronous coordination and global state reconciliation. Define network SLA (latency, availability) as a constraint.

Risk: Redundancy design (hardware and software) may not be modular if Vehicle and Teleoperation do not expose clear interfaces for redundant component activation. Mitigation: Require Vehicle and Teleoperation to implement standardized redundancy interfaces (e.g., 'activate_backup_subsystem', 'query_redundancy_status'). Safety Services orchestrates via these interfaces.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Safety Services achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. This is excellent alignment. The module is the natural owner of system-level availability policy, fault tolerance, and resilience. Minor diffusion (0.08) is acceptable and expected: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement redundant hardware/software and expose health telemetry, but control logic remains centralized. Alternatives (Vehicle, Teleoperation, NGHE Solution) all score poorly (0.35–0.45 containment, 0.55–0.65 diffusion) due to separation-of-concerns violations and latency/coupling risks. Recommendation: Confirm Safety Services as the designated owner and proceed with detailed interface design (Telemetry schema, SafetyAlert format, Tasking protocol, Network Link SLA).

recommended_revisions: Revision 1: Explicitly define 'availability' in acceptance criteria. Clarify whether target ≥0.97 applies to full operational readiness or includes degraded-mode operation (e.g., reduced speed, single-channel control). This affects how redundancy and failover are designed and measured.

Revision 2: Document failover decision latency requirements. Specify maximum acceptable time from fault detection to failover activation (e.g., <100ms for critical subsystems). This drives the choice between synchronous (Safety Services) vs. asynchronous (local autonomy) failover logic.

Revision 3: Define the Telemetry interface schema. Specify which health metrics Vehicle and Teleoperation must expose (e.g., sensor status, subsystem temperature, battery voltage, network latency). This enables Safety Services to implement predictive monitoring consistently.

Revision 4: Define the SafetyAlert interface format. Specify alert severity levels, diagnostic payload, and expected response actions (e.g., 'failover_required', 'component_replacement_needed', 'degraded_mode_active'). This ensures Vehicle and Teleoperation can act on Safety Services directives.

Revision 5: Document network link reliability assumptions. Specify expected latency, packet loss rate, and availability SLA for the Network Link interface. If network is unreliable, local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation becomes critical; Safety Services provides asynchronous coordination.

Revision 6: Clarify redundancy ownership boundaries. Specify which redundant components (hardware and software) are owned by Vehicle vs. Teleoperation vs. Safety Services. For example: Vehicle owns redundant motors and sensors; Teleoperation owns redundant communication channels; Safety Services owns failover orchestration logic.

Revision 7: Add a constraint on Safety Services availability. If Safety Services itself fails, can Vehicle and Teleoperation operate in degraded mode? Define the fallback behavior (e.g., Vehicle defaults to safe state, Teleoperation switches to manual control). This prevents Safety Services from becoming a single point of failure.

Revision 8: Specify the evaluation period and measurement methodology. Define how availability will be measured (e.g., uptime / total time, number of failures, mean-time-between-failures, mean-time-to-repair). Agree on data collection, logging, and reporting procedures.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. Safety Services is confirmed as the designated owner with 0.92 containment and acceptable 0.08 diffusion. The DP (four pillars: redundancy + failover, modular hot-swap, predictive monitoring, fault detection/isolation + diagnostics) is logically sound and measurable. However, the recommended_revisions identify 8 critical clarifications required before detailed design can proceed. Rather than stop prematurely or revise the current level (which would delay decomposition), the optimal path is to decompose FR7 into next-level sub-FRs that explicitly address the open design questions. This ensures that sub-level requirements capture the constraints and assumptions identified in the gate review, preventing diffusion and rework downstream. High safety criticality reinforces the need for explicit sub-level requirements to govern redundancy design, failover latency, interface schemas, and network reliability assumptions.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7.1: Provide standardized health telemetry interface that exposes subsystem-level metrics (sensor status, temperature, voltage, network latency, degradation indicators) in a schema-defined format to enable Safety Services predictive monitoring.

FR7.2: Implement failover decision logic with defined latency bounds (<100ms for critical subsystems) that coordinates redundancy activation across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems while maintaining global availability policy.

FR7.3: Expose redundant hardware and software components (motors, sensors, communication channels, control algorithms) via standardized activation interfaces (e.g., 'activate_backup_subsystem', 'query_redundancy_status') to enable Safety Services orchestration.

FR7.4: Consolidate subsystem-level fault detection and isolation diagnostics into a unified SafetyAlert interface that communicates fault severity, root cause, and required remedial actions (failover, degraded mode, component replacement) to Vehicle and Teleoperation.

FR7.5: Define and enforce network link reliability SLA (latency, packet loss, availability thresholds) and implement local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation to tolerate network transients while Safety Services provides asynchronous coordination and state reconciliation.

FR7.6: Implement fallback behavior for Safety Services unavailability such that Vehicle defaults to safe state and Teleoperation switches to manual control, preventing Safety Services from becoming a single point of failure.

FR7.7: Establish continuous availability measurement and reporting infrastructure (instrumentation, logging, evaluation period definition, baseline/target thresholds) to verify ≥0.97 availability target against agreed acceptance criteria.

revise.why: No revision of current FR7, DP7, or IM (Safety Services) is required. All gates pass. Revisions identified in recommended_revisions are design clarifications that should be captured as constraints and acceptance criteria for the sub-level FRs, not as changes to the current level.

assumptions: Safety Services is the single point of control for system-level availability policy and failover orchestration; Vehicle and Teleoperation implement redundant hardware/software and expose health telemetry but do not independently trigger global failover.
Availability target ≥0.97 is defined as uptime / total evaluation period, with explicit clarification of whether degraded-mode operation (reduced speed, single-channel control) counts as 'available'.
Failover decision latency requirement is <100ms for critical subsystems; this drives choice between synchronous (Safety Services) vs. asynchronous (local autonomy) failover logic.
Network link reliability is sufficient to support asynchronous coordination; if network SLA cannot be met, local failover autonomy in Vehicle and Teleoperation becomes mandatory.
Vehicle and Teleoperation can implement standardized redundancy interfaces and expose health metrics without architectural redesign.
Safety Services availability is not 100%; fallback behavior (Vehicle safe state, Teleoperation manual control) is acceptable when Safety Services is unavailable.
open_risks: Predictive condition monitoring logic may diffuse across Safety Services, Vehicle, and Teleoperation if health metric schema is not rigorously defined and enforced. Mitigation: FR7.1 explicitly requires standardized Telemetry interface; ownership of prediction logic must be documented in DP7 implementation plan.

Failover orchestration may become fragmented if local autonomy boundaries are not clearly defined. Mitigation: FR7.2 requires explicit latency bounds and decision rules per failure mode; Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation must jointly define failover decision authority matrix.

Fault detection and isolation logic may duplicate across Safety Services and Vehicle/Teleoperation if subsystem diagnostics are not integrated. Mitigation: FR7.4 requires unified SafetyAlert interface; ownership of system-level vs. subsystem-level diagnostics must be documented.

Network link reliability may be insufficient to support Safety Services coordination, causing failover delays or inconsistency. Mitigation: FR7.5 requires explicit network SLA definition and local failover autonomy; network performance must be baselined before design finalization.

Safety Services itself may become a single point of failure if fallback behavior is not implemented. Mitigation: FR7.6 explicitly requires fallback behavior; Vehicle and Teleoperation must be capable of safe operation without Safety Services.

Availability measurement methodology may be ambiguous, leading to disputes over whether target is met. Mitigation: FR7.7 requires explicit instrumentation, logging, and acceptance criteria definition; measurement plan must be agreed before evaluation period begins.
evidence_needed: Detailed Telemetry interface schema (data types, update frequency, health metric definitions) agreed by Safety Services, Vehicle, and Teleoperation.
Failover decision latency requirements and decision rules per failure mode (e.g., sensor failure, subsystem timeout, network loss) documented and validated against system-level safety analysis.
Standardized redundancy activation interfaces (API signatures, state machine, error handling) defined and implemented in Vehicle and Teleoperation.
SafetyAlert interface format (severity levels, diagnostic payload, response actions) specified and validated against expected failure scenarios.
Network link SLA (latency, packet loss, availability) baselined and confirmed feasible; local failover autonomy design validated if network SLA cannot be met.
Fallback behavior specification for Safety Services unavailability (Vehicle safe state definition, Teleoperation manual control mode) documented and tested.
Availability measurement plan (instrumentation, logging, evaluation period, baseline/target thresholds, acceptance criteria) agreed and implemented.
Redundancy ownership matrix (which components owned by Vehicle vs. Teleoperation vs. Safety Services) documented and reviewed by all stakeholders.
